title: The main tax, super and other financial changes that start today
full_text:
Explainer The tweaks go well beyond retirement income.
From Wednesday, everybody earning over $45,000 will receive a tax cut of $268 this financial year, rising to $536 in 2027-28 and every year thereafter.
Other pluses starting on July 1 include an increase in the pre-tax super contribution threshold from $30,000 to $32,500, which means you can save more for retirement.
Plus, a lift in the Medicare levy surcharge threshold will allow single individuals to earn up to $105,000 without private cover before facing penalties.
Here are 11 of the key July 1 changes explained.
Tax cutThe tax rate for income between $18,201 and $45,000 falls from 16 per cent to 15 per cent, delivering relief to anyone earning over the $18,200 tax-free threshold.
For workers earning $45,000 or more, this change will save them $268 in tax this financial year, rising to $536 next financial year.
This measure was introduced as part of the 2025 federal budget and will be followed by a further cut to 14 per cent on July 1, 2027.
Minimum wage increasesIn addition, the national minimum wage increases by 4.75 per cent to $26.44 per hour.
For a minimum wage worker doing a 38-hour week, they'll now earn $1004.90 weekly, up from $948.
Super contributions
caps increaseThe amount you can contribute to your super at a 15 per cent concessional tax rate is increasing from $30,000 to $32,500 this financial year.
High earners should note that compulsory employer contributions count towards this cap, leaving less room for salary sacrifice.
In line with this increase, the non-concessional super contribution limit is also going up, from $120,000 to $130,000.
Subsequently, the cap on contributions for those using the three-year "bring forward rule" rises to $390,000 from $360,000.
$1000 tax deduction for work-related expensesWorkers can now opt to get an automatic tax deduction of $1000 for work-related expenses, instead of keeping receipts and claiming for the actual amount of work-related deductions you incur.
Labor has claimed the average worker will save $205 due to this measure, but the majority of workers already claim more than $1000 in work-related expenses. If that's you, the current system of keeping receipts and claiming for your actual expenses is still available.
This change comes into force for the new financial year, so will be applicable for your 2026-27 tax return that you complete in a year's time.
Parental leave gets
a 10-day boostThe government paid parental leave that new parents are entitled to is increasing by 10 days.
This means families now receive up to 130 days (26 weeks, up from 24 weeks) of paid parental leave for children born or adopted on or after July 1, 2026.
The number of days in reserve to share with a partner has also increased from 15 to 20 days.
Medicare levy surcharge thresholds increaseHigh earners without private hospital cover get a reprieve as the income thresholds for the Medicare levy surcharge increase. Single individuals can now earn up to $105,000 before the surcharge kicks in, allowing them to avoid the extra tax for longer if they choose not to hold private insurance.
Payday super beginsBefore now, employers only needed to pay superannuation guarantee contributions to their employee's super accounts quarterly, but employees will now receive their super payments within seven business days of each payday.
The new rules encourage employers to pay super at the same time as they pay wages. This means money ends up in super accounts sooner and has more time to compound.
According to the Association of Superannuation Funds Australia, the change could give the average 25-year-old worker an extra $5000 at retirement.
Super transfer
balance cap risesThe amount of super you can transfer tax-free into retirement phase increases from $2 million to $2.1 million.
The new figure only applies to someone who starts their retirement-phase pension for the first time from July 1.
If you started a retirement-phase pension before now, your transfer balance cap will be between $1.6 million and $2 million, depending on when you started it and how much money you originally moved.
Division 296 super tax startsEarnings on super balances above $3 million will be taxed an extra 15 per cent under the controversial Division 296 tax which comes into force.
That additional tax rate goes up to 25 per cent for earnings on balances over $10 million.
Small business instant asset write-off increasesThe government has made the small business instant asset write-off permanent from July 1, and increased the amount to $20,000.
Businesses with a turnover of less than $10 million a year can claim an immediate deduction of up to $20,000 for the business proportion of an asset.
The write-off can be used for multiple assets - new or second-hand - but they must be first used or installed ready for use this financial year.
Loss carry back
offset returnsThe government has brought back the loss carry back tax offset as part of this year's budget.
From July 1, companies with less than $1 billion in annual turnover will be able to offset a tax loss against tax paid in the prior two financial years.
Since this offset was scrapped in 2023, businesses have instead had to carry forward tax losses to offset profits made in future tax years.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Rebecca Pike
heading: Superannuation and Investments
